Ophthalmic AI

Studies Visualizations Datasets ManuscriptMethods Downloads

Supplementary audit trail

Evidence-gap assignments

Inspect every article-level assignment underlying the general and ethics evidence-gap maps. One publication may contribute to multiple dimensions, so assignment totals are not publication totals.

Interpretation boundary

The evidence-gap maps summarize how publications frame each dimension. They do not represent pooled effect sizes, diagnostic-accuracy estimates, risk-of-bias ratings, or certainty-of-evidence assessments.

Operational definitions

What each evidence-map category filters

Selecting a dimension in an assignment table retains publications that meet its evidence required criterion. Evidence described as not sufficient is filtered out and does not create an assignment. These are the operational definitions reported in the current supplementary category-rules table.

General-map dimensions

Positive means that the publication frames AI as enabling or improving the named dimension. Negative means that it frames AI as creating or exposing a barrier, risk, failure, or constraint. Balanced requires explicit qualifying evidence in both directions within that dimension.

Model performance
Evidence required
Reported quantitative or qualitative performance, comparison, error, agreement, calibration, or robustness outcome.
Not sufficient
Architecture or method description without an evaluated outcome.
Technological limitations
Evidence required
An explicit AI/model/system failure mode, generalization or robustness constraint, image artifact, technical restriction, or a method that explicitly overcomes such a constraint.
Not sufficient
A limitation inferred from study design, a sample-size or data limitation without a stated technical consequence, or generic future work.
Real-world impact
Evidence required
A deployed or actually used system, or a measured patient, clinician, workflow, referral, treatment, access, or service consequence.
Not sufficient
Retrospective benchmark performance, claimed clinical relevance, prospective utility, or an unmeasured promise of workload reduction.
Data availability, integration, quality, and standardization
Evidence required
An explicit AI-linked effect of data scarcity, missingness, quality, representativeness, annotation, access, harmonization, integration, interoperability, or standardization.
Not sufficient
Merely naming or using a dataset, an ordinary train/test split or preprocessing step, or a routine data-availability statement.
Infrastructural demands
Evidence required
An explicit deployment or integration burden or enabler involving hardware, cloud or network capacity, connectivity, staffing, workflow, maintenance, or institutional resources.
Not sufficient
A list of devices, software, GPUs, memory, or runtime in the methods section without a stated implementation consequence.
Financial costs
Evidence required
An explicit consequence involving monetary cost, affordability, reimbursement, economic burden, staffing costs, or health-system resources.
Not sufficient
Parameter count, runtime, hardware specification, or "computational cost" without a monetary or affordability consequence.
Regulatory requirements
Evidence required
An explicit AI-related approval, certification, liability, legal, compliance, governance, or postmarket obligation or barrier.
Not sufficient
Routine research ethics approval, consent, Declaration of Helsinki compliance, or adherence to reporting guidelines.
Cybersecurity risks
Evidence required
An explicit hostile attack, vulnerability, security safeguard, adversarial threat, or resilience claim involving an AI or data system.
Not sufficient
Generic privacy, ordinary model robustness, access details, or an inferred security risk.
Ethical concerns
Evidence required
An explicit cross-cutting AI-related ethical harm, benefit, dilemma, or responsible-AI implication.
Not sufficient
Routine ethics approval or consent, or an ethical issue supported only by procedural boilerplate.

Ethics-map dimensions

Caused means that the publication frames AI as introducing or amplifying the issue. Resolved requires a reported AI-related mechanism that mitigates the issue. Caused and resolved requires explicit evidence in both directions.

Privacy
Evidence required
An explicit AI-linked privacy risk or a concrete privacy-specific AI mechanism that is reported as mitigating it.
Not sufficient
Routine anonymization, de-identification, consent, restricted data access, or a non-public dataset without an explicit AI-related privacy effect.
Transparency
Evidence required
An explicit effect on explainability, interpretability, disclosure, traceability, or clinician understanding of an AI decision.
Not sufficient
Routine methods reporting, performance metrics, model diagrams, heatmaps, or visualization without a stated interpretive function.
Integrity
Evidence required
An explicit effect on truthfulness, authenticity, manipulation, corruption, or evidentiary integrity of AI/data outputs.
Not sufficient
Ordinary accuracy, ground-truth labeling, validation, reproducibility, data cleaning, or data-quality methods.
Security
Evidence required
An explicit ethical threat or protection affecting the confidentiality, integrity, or availability of an AI or data system.
Not sufficient
Generic privacy, robustness, login/access details, or a security effect inferred by the reviewer.
Non-maleficence
Evidence required
An explicit AI-related harm, safety risk, consequence of an incorrect output, or reported harm-prevention mechanism.
Not sufficient
A generic diagnostic purpose, accuracy improvement, or patient benefit inferred without a stated harm connection.
Trust
Evidence required
Trust, confidence, acceptance, reliance, or credibility of the AI system is explicitly named, investigated, or measured.
Not sufficient
Trust inferred solely from accuracy, robustness, expert agreement, validation, or explainability.
Accountability
Evidence required
Explicit responsibility, liability, oversight, contestability, or answerability for an AI-supported decision.
Not sufficient
Author contribution or accountability statements, ethics approval, or expert labeling alone.
Equity
Evidence required
An explicit AI-related subgroup fairness, bias, disparity, representativeness, access, or distributional effect.
Not sufficient
Ordinary class balancing unless linked to affected patient groups, access, or outcome inequity.
Autonomy
Evidence required
An explicit effect on patient or clinician agency, informed choice about AI use, or meaningful human control.
Not sufficient
The technical term "autonomous", generic personalization, or clinician involvement without an agency effect.

General dimensions

1,049 assignments across nine dimensions, categorized as positive, negative, or balanced according to each publication's framing.

CSVJSON
